heading: VERSO TUTTE LE CREATURE
card_summary: This chunk opens the third Tone of the first Canticle with two closely related chapters on the arithmetic and theological foundations of universal harmony. Chapter 1 explains how the monad unfolds into the number twenty-seven — the cube of three — following Plato's Timaeus (as interpreted by Proclus and Calcidius), with Zorzi adding a Kabbalistic dimension by identifying Hebrew tohu/wa-vohu (Genesis 1:2) and hiyyuli with primordial matter (Greek hyle, Latin chaos). Chapter 2 argues that the trinary structure of all creation reflects the Triune God, marshalling Pythagoras, Aristotle's De caelo, Hermes Trismegistus (heimarmene, necessity, order), Wisdom 11:20 (number, weight, measure), Paul's Acts 17:28, Jeremiah 23:24, the Zoroastrian triad Oromasi/Mitri/Aramini, Plato's Second Letter to Dionysius, the Kabbalistic letter bet (binah/house as the Son's seal), and the Hermetic sphere-metaphor, all converging on the claim that from the Triune Archetype a triple-structured cosmos necessarily proceeds through three enneads — angelic, celestial, and elemental.
essay:
## Overview

Pages 411–421 constitute the opening of what Zorzi calls the exposition of the *concordia trium Enneadum* — the harmonic concord of the three novenaries that structure created reality. The two chapters present a dense synthesis of Platonic arithmetic, Kabbalistic exegesis of Genesis, Pythagorean cosmology, Hermetic trinitarian theology, and Pauline-Aristotelian natural philosophy. The driving thesis is that the One necessarily unfolds into multiplicity through the ternary principle, and that this unfolding carries an intrinsic harmonic structure all the way from the Archetype to the lowest material things.

---

## Chapter 1: The Monad Unfolding into Twenty-Seven

Zorzi opens with the problem inherited from Plato's *Timaeus* (and its Pythagorean source Timaeus of Locri): how can the monad's unity cohere with multiplicity without fracture? The answer is harmonic proportion. Following Plato, Zorzi notes that the World-Soul was constructed from the monad through double and triple ratios until the number twenty-seven (3³, the cube of the first number) was reached. Proclus and Calcidius, he notes, substitute the number six (the first perfect number) for the unit, reaching 162 by the same doubling-tripling procedure, in order to fill all the musical intervals — fifths, octaves, tones, semitones, and lemmas — so that the entire Soul and World are perfectly harmonized.

> *«La monade, prima e suprema, si diffonde nel primo numero, cioè il ternario; quest'ultimo, elevato al quadrato, riempie il novenario... Lo stesso numero, elevato al cubo, produce appunto il già ricordato ventisette.»*
<cite>p. 411</cite>

Before these harmonic grades can be distributed, Zorzi insists on the prior necessity of *materia prima*. He gives the term in four languages: Latin *chaos*, Greek *hyle*, and Hebrew *hiyyuli* (היולי) and *tohu* (תהו). The parallel to Genesis 1:1–2 is then developed: Moses, says Zorzi, "preceded the creation of the world with the production of matter" when he wrote *In principio creavit Deus caelum et terram* — heaven and earth as raw materials, subsequently formed on the second and third days respectively.

A notable exegetical point concerns the Hebrew word *arachiha* (רקיע, *raqia'*): Zorzi insists it does not mean *firmamentum* (as the Vulgate has it) but rather the globe and mass of *all* the heavens, citing as proof that Genesis says God placed the two great luminaries *in arachiha* — they cannot be in the starry firmament alone, since each is in its own sphere.

> *«In effetti arachiha non significa "firmamento", come si legge nella traduzione comune, ma il globo, la massa di tutti i cieli... da cui si desume che arachiha designa collettivamente tutte le sfere.»*
<cite>p. 412</cite>

The Hebrew *wa-vohu* (ובהו) is glossed philosophically: "and in which was already present what was to take shape," meaning that all forms pre-exist in matter as potencies before they are unfolded. The twenty-seven primary genera of created things are divided into three novenaries — supracelestial, celestial, and elemental — whose harmonic concord descends from the Archetype.

Technical musical terminology is integrated throughout: *tonus*, *semitonium*, *lemma* (half-semitone), *quinta*, and *octava*. The ratio 9:8 (the tone) dividing into semitone and lemma by multiplying 8 and 9 by 2 and 4 (yielding 16, 32, 18, 36) is presented as the model for how even the "natural number" (i.e., the proportional measure of things) can be subdivided within species.

---

## Chapter 2: The Trinary Progress from the Triune God through All Creatures

The second chapter argues that Pythagoras drew his doctrine of the ternary not only from Chaldean and Assyrian sources but from the *sapienza più arcana degli ebrei* (the more occult wisdom of the Hebrews). Three is the foundation of all arithmetic growth (length, breadth, depth) and of solid geometry (the cube admits no further dimensional addition). Zorzi cites Aristotle's *De caelo* I.1 directly:

> *«Il numero ternario lo abbiamo ricevuto dalla natura e ci atteniamo a esso come a una legge»*
<cite>p. 416 (Aristotle, De caelo I.1)</cite>

All spiritual and corporeal realities consist of beginning, middle, and end — a ternary structure they derive from the supreme Triad "from whom, in whom, and through whom all things exist" (Paul, Romans 11:36 / Acts 17:28). Hermes Trismegistus is cited for the triad *heimarmene* (εἱμαρμένη), necessity, and order as the three elements composing the world. Wisdom 11:20 ("number, weight, and measure") is mapped onto the three Persons: number comes from the Father as supreme unity and source of all numbers; weight from the Son as divine Wisdom that weighs all things; measure from the Holy Spirit as the Artisan's spirit distributing proper portions.

The Zoroastrian/Mazdean triad *Oromasim, Mitrim, Araminim* (Ahura Mazda, Mithra, Ahriman — or in Zorzi's theological reading: God, Mind, Soul) is adduced as pagan confirmation of the trinitarian structure of reality.

Plato's cryptic Second Letter to Dionysius ("Around the King of all things are all things, and everything exists for his sake, and he is the cause of all beautiful things") is decoded as a Trinitarian statement: the King's unity binds all things, his grace weighs all things, his ordering is principle, middle, and end of all — an echo of the Orphic formula that "all things depart from him, tend toward him, and find rest and sustenance in him."

A pivotal Kabbalistic identification follows: the Son/divine Wisdom's "seal" in Hebrew theology is the letter *bet* (ב), meaning *bait* (house) — the first letter of *Bereshit* and of the Torah — because all things "dwell" in the Son as ideas (a reference to the Platonic and Kabbalistic doctrine of the Logos/Hokhmah as the locus of divine ideas). All things are then unfolded into their proper forms by the Holy Spirit, which is why the whole creation is described as the work of *Elohim* — a name Zorzi, against some interpreters, insists is grammatically singular (evidenced by its vowel-pointing) though connected with *binah* (intelligence), the sefirah attributed to the Holy Spirit. He cites Ecclesiasticus 39:6 ("and the spirit of understanding shall fill him") in confirmation.

> *«Il sigillo del Figlio e della sapienza divina (secondo la teologia degli ebrei) è ב, bet, ovvero bait, che significa 'casa'. Tutte le cose... assumono le forme loro proprie in virtù dello Spirito santo.»*
<cite>p. 418</cite>

The Hermetic sphere-metaphor — "God is a sphere whose center (the Father) is everywhere by his power, and whose circumference (the Son, divine Wisdom) is nowhere, because it cannot be contained though it embraces all things" — closes the theological argument, with Wisdom of Sirach 24 ("I circled the vault of heaven alone and penetrated into the depths of the abyss") cited as scriptural parallel.

The cosmological conclusion follows: from a Triune Archetype the fabric of creation must necessarily proceed in a trinary and triquetrous order. The simple ternary is attributed to the simplest God (one unity unfolding into three hypostases). The squared ternary (nine) distributes the three enneads of intelligences, heavens, and corruptible-generatable things, all perfected by a tenth (a sacramental mystery deferred to a later place). The cubed ternary (twenty-seven) contains all together, and in it all musical proportions are enclosed — as Plato hints and Proclus expounds at length.

---

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This chunk establishes the arithmetic and theological scaffolding on which the rest of the *De Harmonia Mundi* will rest. The three enneads — angelic, celestial, elemental — are announced as the structure to be explored in the chapters immediately following (Chapter 3 opens with the nine orders of angels). The integration of Genesis exegesis (*arachiha*, *tohu*, *wa-vohu*, *Elohim*), Kabbalistic letter-symbolism (bet/binah), Platonic world-soul arithmetic (the lambda series to 27), Pythagorean musical ratios (tone, semitone, lemma), Hermetic trinitarian theology, and Zoroastrian cosmology is characteristic of Zorzi's *prisca theologia* method: all traditions converge on a single harmonic truth that Scripture alone fully reveals.
